title: Is Blockholder Diversity Detrimental?
abstract: We find that, overall, blockholder diversity, i.e., the firm shareholder base including several different types of blocks, is detrimental to firm performance. We show that lagged disclosure, on exogenous predetermined dates, that reveals an increase in block diversity is followed by a negative market reaction. Firms held by heterogeneous blockholders consistently perform worse than firms held by homogeneous blockholders. Block diversity is particularly detrimental when uncertainty is high. Disagreement among shareholders (e.g., as reflected in the frequency of lawsuits being filed) increases when the blockholder base is diverse. We make our blockholder data set public for the benefit of other researchers.

author.Miriam Schwartz-Ziv: Business School, Hebrew University of Jerusalem and European Corporate Governance Institute, Jerusalem 9190500, Israel
author.Ekaterina Volkova: Department of Finance, University of Melbourne, Parkville, Victoria 3053, Australia

article.1. Introduction: Different types of blocks frequently coexist within a firm. The literature on blockholders typically investigates how the presence of one or more specific type of blockholder1or the presence of multiple financial blockholders2is related to firm performance. Our paper focuses on a different element of the shareholder base: We investigate the relation between block diversity, that is, whether the firm’s shareholder base includes multiple types of blocks and firm value. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first paper to squarely focus on this question, perhaps because obtaining data on nonfinancial blocks is challenging. For our analysis, we constructed a blockholder data set that includes the nonfinancial blocks, and we also make this data set public for other researchers to use. Holderness (2003),Cronqvist and Fahlenbrach (2009), andEdmans and Holderness (2017)demonstrate how different types of blockholders can govern in different ways. For example, although governance can be conducted via exit or voice (Admati and Pfleiderer 2009,Edmans 2009), financial institutions frequently monitor through exit (Parrino et al. 2003,McCahery et al. 2016), whereas nonfinancial blocks frequently govern via voice (seeBrav et al. (2008)andKlein and Zur (2009)with respect to hedge funds). Prior studies from the board literature and the team diversity literature have shown that diversity can be beneficial when different perspectives are needed, such as in complex environments (Jehn et al. 1999), but can also be detrimental when there is no need for different perspectives (Pelled et al. 1999). Correspondingly, on the one hand, block diversity may potentially enhance firm performance, because different types of blocks may complement each other or cross-monitor each other (Edmans and Manso 2011,Dhillon and Rossetto 2015). On the other hand, block diversity may be detrimental to firm performance.Hadlock and Schwartz-Ziv (2019)show that certain types of blocks repel each other, suggesting that block heterogeneity may be costly.Li et al. (2022)show that after shareholders observe vote outcomes, they are more likely to sell their shares if they disagree with the vote outcome, implying that shareholder diversity is costly. Thus, we present one hypothesis that posits that block diversity enhances firm performance and present a competing hypothesis that posits that block diversity is detrimental to firm performance. Overall, we find support for the latter possibility: firms with a diverse blockholder base, which we measure by the number of types of blockholders holding the firm, have weaker financial performance. Different types of blockholders are active and govern in different ways (Israelsen et al. 2022). Our results suggest that having different types of blockholders pulling simultaneously toward different and conflicting governance practices can be detrimental for the firm. To measure block diversity, we manually assemble a unique panel data set that covers the universe of blockholders (i.e., shareholders holding at least 5% of the firm’s outstanding shares) of all publicly traded firms in the United States during the 1998–2016 period for which CRSP/Compustat data are available. Our data set is based on the universe of 13D and 13G filings and forms 3, 4, and 5 (further details are provided in Online Appendix A, part 1). We further refine the ownership of the institutional blocks using Thomson Reuters. We measure block diversity by the number of different types of blockholders. Block positions are classified into four types depending on whether they are owned by a financial institution, hedge fund, individual, or other private entity.3Our data set document that U.S. firms frequently have a diverse blockholder base: on average, 47% of the firms are held by at least two types of blockholders. The number of blocks in firms has been increasing over time, but block diversity has been decreasing. These somewhat contradictory trends motivate us to investigate whether block diversity is detrimental to firm performance. We exploit the reality that blockholders purchasing a block usually disclose their block purchase for the first time on a random predetermined date (February 14) in a 13G filing, which is filed between 45 to 410 calendar days after the actual block is purchased. Put differently, the disclosure of the purchase of the block typically occurs many months after the block is actually purchased.4Generally, an analysis of the market’s response to a filing revealing a recent block purchase does not allow disentangling the market’s response to the filing from its response to other events that may have triggered the blockholders’ entry (e.g., chief executive officer (CEO) resignation), because these events are essentially concurrent. However, in our analysis, we are able to identify the specific response to the revelation of the new blockholder base revealed on the filing date because we focus on the filing date that reveals the block purchase, a date that typically occurs many months after the actual purchase occurred. The analysis also accounts for the number of blockholders in the firm to assure that the results are not driven by this potentially omitted variable. We find that when an increase in block diversity is revealed in a filing made on the previously noted predetermined date, the market’s response is 0.9% more negative (during the [0, 5] window) than when a filing reveals that a homogeneous blockholder base has been maintained. This finding suggests that the market views an increase in block diversity as being detrimental to firm performance. Our result holds when we limit the sample to entries of a particular block type and/or when we limit the sample to firms that already have a particular block type (i.e., financial institution, hedge fund, individual, or other private entity) at the time a new block joins the blockholder base. The results are also robust when we account for the magnitude of the block type diversity and for whether the entering block has prior industry experience. In various corporate settings, for example, in the context of board composition, prior literature has documented that one (board) composition may be optimal for one firm, but a different composition may be optimal for a different firm. For example,Coles et al. (2008)find evidence that for complex firms, which typically have greater advising needs, outside directors are particularly beneficial.5Such studies suggest that also in our setting one size may not fit all, meaning that block diversity may not be universally detrimental toallfirms. Indeed, we show that block diversity is detrimental (neutral) for firms that face high (low) uncertainty/volatility. This finding is consistent with studies that have found that in environments with high uncertainty, homogeneous teams/boards are particularly beneficial (Bernile et al. 2018), potentially because they counterbalance the unstable environment. In addition, we show that block diversity is costly (neutral) for small companies with short 10-K filings (large companies with long 10-K filings). These findings are consistent with studies that have found that for simple routine (complicated) tasks, a homogeneous (heterogeneous) team will maximize team performance (Jehn et al. 1999,Bowers et al. 2000), because in such settings, there is no benefit to having a diverse skill set. Thus, block diversity is neutral for some companies, detrimental for other companies, and overall, on the aggregate level, it is detrimental. To understand why block diversity is detrimental to firm performance we turn to prior studies that have documented that when different types of blocks/shareholders coexist, disagreement and conflict arises (Hadlock and Schwartz-Ziv 2019,Levit et al. 2021,Li et al. 2022). We find evidence supporting this narrative: First, aggregate vote outcomes of shareholder votes demonstrate that as block diversity increases, shareholders are significantly less likely to vote in agreement with each other or with management. Votes cast at the mutual fund level demonstrate that funds are more likely to vote against management when a nonfinancial block is present, suggesting that the presence of a blockholder that is not of your own type enhances disagreement. In a second analysis, we uniquely assemble a data set that documents lawsuits filed by shareholders that involve a blockholder. Such lawsuits can reflect whether conflict exists among the shareholder base. We find that such lawsuits are significantly more likely to be filed when the blockholder base is diverse. This finding also suggests that when different types of blockholders coexist in the blockholder base, shareholders are more likely to experience conflict. Examining settings that allow for exogenous changes to block diversity (revelations) enables us to isolate factors that may affect both formations of blocks and firm performance. However, we also examine whether the complete sample indicates that a diverse blockholder base is associated with lower firm performance. Although such an analysis does not address endogeneity concerns, it enables us to observe general patterns in the complete sample and is not confined to a subset of observations of firms that experienced an exogenous change to their block diversity. To conduct such an analysis, we create portfolios for which we take a long position on firms that have a diverse blockholder base (i.e., firms that are held by at least two different types of blockholders) and a short position on firms that have a homogeneous blockholder base (i.e., firms that are held by only one type of blockholder). We find that such value-weighted (equal-weighted) portfolios produce a nonnegligible annual abnormal return equal to 3.78% (11.08%). In addition, in a separate analysis, we examine (for all firm and year observations) firm performance one, two, and three years following the observed blockholder base. We find that, relative to firms with a homogeneous blockholder base, firms with a heterogeneous blockholder base have a smaller Tobin’s Q, profit margin, and Return on Assets (ROA) one to three years following the blockholder base at a given year. At minimum, these results demonstrate that firms with a heterogeneous blockholder base are in fact less profitable than firms with a homogeneous blockholder base. One may well wonder why firms with a diverse blockholder base exist in the first place. AsHadlock and Schwartz-Ziv (2019, table 2) show, blockholders consider a large number of factors when determining in which firms to invest, for example, the firm’s industry, characteristics, and location. The composition of the blockholder base is only one of several important factors considered by (potential) blockholders when deciding in which firms they wish to hold block positions. Additionally, as the fraction of assets invested in an index fund increases (Chinco and Sammon 2022), institutional investors have less discretion as to where to invest their assets and thus may ultimately invest large stakes in firms in which it may be suboptimal to do so. To conclude, in our study, we manually construct a unique data set covering not only financial block positions but also nonfinancial block positions in all U.S. public companies between 1998 and 2016. To the best of our knowledge, this data set is the most comprehensive and up-to-date data set documenting block-level ownership in publicly listed firms in the United States that has been used in an academic study related to block ownership.6We make this data set publicly available for all researchers, and our hope is that this data set will facilitate other studies investigating topics related to block ownership.7In addition to making this data set public, we make two contributions. The first and main one is to demonstrate that overall, block diversity is detrimental to firm performance. To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to investigate the relation between block diversity and firm performance. Our second contribution is to demonstrate that firms with a diverse blockholder base are more likely to experience disagreement and conflict among shareholders. This increased level of conflict may explain why firm performance suffers when block ownership is diverse.
article.2. Hypothesis and Data: 2.1. Literature and Hypothesis 2.1.1. Diversity in Prior Corporate Finance Studies. Holderness (2003),Cronqvist and Fahlenbrach (2009), andEdmans and Holderness (2017)all point out that blockholders can be of different types and govern in different ways.8Perhaps a simple distinction that demonstrates block diversity is financial versus nonfinancial blocks.Edmans (2009)andAdmati and Pfleiderer (2009)emphasize that governance can occur through voice (i.e., monitoring and intervention) or, alternatively, exit or the threat of exit (i.e., trade).Parrino et al. (2003)andMcCahery et al. (2016)find that financial institutions tend to use exit as their governance mechanism. By contrast, nonfinancial blocks frequently govern via voice (seeBrav et al. (2008)andKlein and Zur (2009)with respect to hedge funds).Israelsen et al. (2022)provide further support for the conclusion that financial blocks tend to govern via exit, whereas nonfinancial blocks tend to govern via voice. InSection 2.3, we discuss and demonstrate finer categories of block types and a corresponding measure of block diversity. Thus, different types of blockholders may govern using different mechanisms and have different philosophies on how it is best to govern. Following up this observation, our paper addresses the question of whether block diversity is detrimental to firm performance. To address this question that focuses on block diversity, we start by addressing the broader literature on diversity. In general, diversity potentially entails both costs and benefits. These costs and benefits have been revealed in prior academic literature in several contexts, for example, with respect to team diversity and board diversity. In this context,Jehn et al. (1999)andBowers et al. (2000)show that when faced with a complex task requiring a diverse skill set, a diverse team frequently outperforms a homogeneous one. Conversely,Pelled et al. (1999)andBrief et al. (2005)find that increasing diversity (e.g., in terms of race and tenure) can increase conflict among group members and therefore be detrimental to group performance. A similar notion is conveyed in the literature on board diversity, which is surveyed comprehensively byFerreira (2010).Ferreira (2010)points out the advantages of diversity, for example, benefiting from different perspectives and having more connections, but also points out the potential costs, for example, experiencing more conflict, lack of cooperation, and insufficient communication. Accordingly, it is perhaps not surprising that some of the board diversity literature has documented a positive relation between board diversity and financial performance (Erhardt et al. 2003,Bernile et al. 2018), whereas other studies have documented a negative relation or shown that board diversity can hurt board performance (Adams et al. 2018,Donaldson et al. 2020). 2.1.2. Block Diversity Being Beneficial. Block diversity may or may not be beneficial.9On the one hand, block diversity may enhance firm performance, because different types of blocks may complement each other; that is, firm value increases as the number of block types increases. For example,Dhillon and Rossetto (2015)show that block diversity can enhance firm value if different types of blocks cross-monitor each other.Edmans and Manso (2011)demonstrate that multiple blocks, which can be of different types, monitor effectively because they compete to collect valuable information that they use for their trading decisions, thereby contributing to a stock price with higher precision.Brav et al. (2019)demonstrate that block diversity, which, in their context, consists of an activist hedge fund teaming with institutional investors to form a “wolf pack” led by the activist, can collectively and effectively engage with management.10 Additionally,Cvijanović et al. (2022)show that blockholders (e.g., mutual funds) who observe an exit of an informed blockholder (e.g., a hedge fund) will likely follow suit and start to sell their own stocks, ultimately pressuring the company to improve its actions.Song (2017)shows that a diverse blockholder base, for example, one that includes a passive and an active blockholder, may strengthen the active blockholder’s incentive to intervene (because the passive blockholder is not likely to do so), thereby contributing to governance. 2.1.3. Detrimental Block Diversity. The second and opposite possibility is that block diversity is detrimental to firm performance. For example,Cronqvist and Fahlenbrach (2009)andEdmans and Holderness (2017)argue that different types of blocks may have different preferences and beliefs. These studies suggest that blockholders may disagree on what goals the firm should aim to achieve and what actions it should take to achieve those goals.11An example that illustrates this possibility can be found inBarnea and Rubin (2010), who show that individual ownership is negatively related to corporate social responsibility (CSR) ratings, whereas institutional ownership is unrelated to CSR ratings. They interpret these findings as demonstrating that different types of shareholders have conflicting goals.12Additionally,Hadlock and Schwartz-Ziv (2019)show that different types of blocks, in particular, financial and nonfinancial blocks, repel each other, suggesting that coexistence of different block types may be costly. However,Hadlock and Schwartz-Ziv (2019)focus on theblockholders’ decision to coexist or not to coexist with another block. We focus on the market’s perspective on block diversity. The market may or may not view blockholder diversity the same way blockholders view it. Several studies have documented a negative relation between a certain element of shareholder diversity and profitability. For example,Maury and Pajuste (2005)show that a relatively (un)equal distribution of votes among large blockholders has a (negative) positive effect on firm value.Laeven and Levine (2008)document a negative relationship between cash-flow rights dispersion and Tobin’s Q.Kandel et al. (2011)show that Swedish firms whose small investors are of similar age, wealth, and location have higher profitability and returns.Levit et al. (2021)demonstrate that trading aligns the shareholder base with the expected outcome, thereby suggesting that shareholders with similar governance preferences tend to cluster together.Li et al. (2022)show that after shareholders observe vote outcomes, they are more likely to sell their shares if they disagree with the vote outcome, implying that shareholder diversity is costly. These studies imply that shareholder diversity is costly, and that a given shareholder will typically prefer to invest in a firm that has a large percentage of shareholders who agree with the given shareholder on what actions the firm should or should not take. The findings ofLi et al. (2022)demonstrate that when such an agreement does not exist, and the views of the given shareholder do not align with those of most other shareholders, the given shareholder is more likely to choose to sell his shares. This choice suggests that a diverse shareholder/blockholder base is costly. Taken together, the studies surveyed in this section lead to two competing hypotheses. Block diversity is detrimental to firm performance. Block diversity enhances firm performance. We examine these two competing and opposite hypotheses in subsequent sections. 2.2. Data We construct a unique panel data set that records block positions for U.S. publicly listed firms for the 1998–2016 period. A block position is defined as a position that is equal to at least 5% of the firm’s outstanding shares. A shareholder who purchases a block that consists of at least 5% of the firm’s outstanding shares is required to file either a 13D or a 13G filing.13Form 13D is more detailed than form 13G because it includes the item “Purpose of Transaction,” in which the blockholder must specify the purpose of his transaction. Additionally, form 13D must be filed within 10 calendar days from the day the block was purchased, whereas form 13G must be filed within 45 days after the end of the calendar year in which the block was purchased, that is, a substantial gap. The 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 rule specifies which blockholder may file a 13G filing, as opposed to a 13D filing. It states that a blockholder may file a 13G if the blockholder “has not acquired the securities with any purpose, or with the effect, of changing or influencing the control of the issuer, or in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having that purpose or effect….” Thus, the latter quote suggests that 13D blocks may at least have the intention of being more involved relative to 13G blocks, even though the rule does not rely on any measurable criteria, and therefore leaves room for interpretation on who is ultimately required to file a 13D. Ultimately, 85% of all blockholders file a 13G form, as opposed to a 13D form, that is, easily the majority of the filings. Amendments to both types of forms must be filed at least once a year if substantial changes occur.14Forms 13D and 13G and their amendments are all filed through the Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval system (EDGAR). The start year of the sample coincides with the earliest available reliable data on block positions. More specifically, EDGAR required electronic filings starting from 1994; however, from a comparison we conducted between the blockholders documented in the EDGAR 13G and 13D filings and the blockholders documented in the Compact Disclosure data set (available from 1992 to 2006), we conclude that the EDGAR data set is reliable starting from 1998, and therefore start our sample from that year. We construct our data set by first downloading from EDGAR all the 13D and 13G forms and their amendments filed between 1998 and 2016, and then removing all duplicated filings and filings triggered by stock buybacks. The 13D and 13G filings follow Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) guidelines and have a similar structure; however, the exact wording of the form may vary across filings. We develop a set of custom parser scripts that account for this variation in filings’ templates, which allow us to extract the name and CUSIP of the company, the blockholder name, the size of the block position, and the filing date. Additionally, we include in our data set all blocks revealed in a form 3, 4, or 5 (which are the forms used by insiders and shareholders who hold more than 10% of a firm’s shares) and not revealed in a 13G/13D/13GA/13DA filing. In theory, anytime an insider blockholder buys or sells a 5% block, this transaction should be reflected both in a form 3, 4, or 5 and in a 13G, 13D, 13GA, or 13DA filing. However, upon comparing the data to the sample we manually coded from proxy filings, we discover that some insiders did not file a 13G, 13D, 13GA, or 13DA filing but did file a form 3, 4, or 5. In some cases, forms 3, 4, and 5 include multiple shareholders who filed a joint filing. We keep only the filings in which, aggregately, all shareholders included in the filing held more than 5% of the firm’s outstanding shares, and none of these shareholders revealed their block position in the firm by filing a 13G, 13D, 13GA, or 13DA. Ultimately, 3.4% of the block-company-year observations in our data set are obtained from forms 3, 4, and 5. Online Appendix A (part 1) provides further details on the construction of the data set. In Online Appendix A (part 2), for a sample of 75 companies, we compare our data set to blockholding data we manually coded from proxy statements. As we report in Online Appendix A (part 2), a manual inspection of a sample of our data set shows that the accuracy rate of our data set is 96% and that this accuracy rate can quite easily be increased to 98.7% by replacing the 13F blocks in our data set with the blocks reported in Thomson Reuters. We made the data set on block ownership publicly available, and we hope that it will be useful for researchers. The data set is at the blockholder-company-year level and can be obtained directly from WRDS, and the code for assembling the data set is posted on the second author’s GitHub (seeEndnote 8for further details). The data set is at the blockholder-company-year level, and documents for each blockholder-firm-year observation the blockholder name, blockholder Central Index Key (CIK), firm CIK, firm name, year, percentage of the company held by the blockholder, and an indicator for whether the block files a 13F. To further refine our data, for the analysis conducted in the paper, we replace the 13F blockholders from our data set with 13F blockholders reported in Thomson Reuters as of the end of December for each calendar year. If we use our data set without performing the latter replacement, the results remain essentially the same as the ones reported in the paper. The blockholder data set we posted online has already been used in several publications, includingAkins et al. (2020)andMitton (2022). We match the blockholder data set to the CRSP/Compustat merged database. We include only firms traded on the NYSE, NASDAQ, or AMEX exchanges that filed at least one annual report with the SEC during the sample period. In addition, we require that the firms have nonmissing values for total assets, sales, total liabilities, total equity, net income, and operating income in both the observation year and the following year. We use the CRSP Daily Stock File to estimate company returns during the previous year and idiosyncratic volatility. In total, our data set includes 20,913 unique blockholders in 10,436 unique companies. Finally, we obtain total institutional ownership information from Thomson Reuters, shareholder voting from Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS) voting analytics, and data on lawsuits filed from the Westlaw data set. 2.3. Measuring Block Diversity and Summary Statistics Our measure of block diversity is the number of different types of blockholders that hold a block that is equal to at least 5% of the firm’s outstanding shares. Prior literature has suggested different categories for defining different types of blocks (Cronqvist and Fahlenbrach 2009,Holderness 2009,Hadlock and Schwartz-Ziv 2019). Inspired by these prior studies that each use somewhat different block type categories that, nevertheless, do have certain similarities, we define four distinct types of blocks: (1)Financial institution—blockholders that file a 13F form, but are not hedge funds as defined in the next category; (2)Hedge fund, that is, blockholders identified as hedge funds inBrav et al. (2009,2015)15; (3)Individual, that is, blockholders classified as individuals by filing under items 12 and 14 in 13G and 13D filings, respectively, or those that file forms 3, 4, or 5; and (4)Other private entity, that is, blockholders that do not file a 13F filing and are not classified as individuals. Each of these block types may include a diverse mix of shareholders/blockholders (e.g., financial institution blocks may be comprised of both active and passive funds, and individual blocks may be comprised of both insiders and outsiders). Nevertheless, as we will show, each of these block types has unique characteristics relative to the other block types. In addition, prior studies have highlighted the unique characteristics of each of these four types of blocks. For example, financial institutions frequently tend to govern via exit (Parrino et al. 2003,McCahery et al. 2016), whereas hedge funds are more likely to have intense discussions with management and promote the appointment of directors who represent their interests (Brav et al. 2008,Klein and Zur 2009). Individuals face fewer agency problems since they invest their own assets in the firm and are likely to be more hands on and involved in the daily operations of the firm (Agrawal and Knoeber 1996,Himmelberg et al. 1999,Andres 2008). The last group of blockholders, “other private entities,” consists primarily of private and public firms. Prior studies have suggested that such types of blocks are often formed as part of a product–market relationship (Allen and Phillips 2000,Fee et al. 2006). Panel A ofTable 1reports summary statistics at the blockholder-company-year level and demonstrates that the blockholder types we define correspond to other characteristics of block diversity, examined in prior studies, that capture shareholders’ preferences and governance styles (as detailed later). For example, we observe that the average block size of different block types varies: It is equal to 8.12% and 10.5% for financial institutions and hedge funds, respectively, but it is substantially larger—16.2% and 15.42%—for individuals and other private entities, respectively. These figures indicate that the different blockholder types we use also correspond to other variables of block diversity that have been examined in prior studies. These differences further imply that each of the four types of blockholders defined previously has distinct characteristics, preferences, and governance patterns that are not necessarily in sync with those of the other block types. All variables are defined inAppendix Aand are winsorized at the 1% level. Panel B ofTable 1reports that the average (median) number of blocks in a firm is 2.89 (3), demonstrating that firms typically have multiple blocks. In fact, as reported in Panel B ofTable 1, 72% (52.0%) of the firms are held by at least two (three) blocks.Figure 1(which includes the same sample) reports the distribution of the average number of blocks per firm by year.Figure 1(a)demonstrates that, overall, the average number of blocks in U.S. public companies has been gradually increasing over the years from 2.6 in 1998 to 3.5 in 2016.Figure 1(b)reports an overall increase in average total block ownership from 30.6% of the firm’s outstanding shares in 1998% to 36.2% in 2016. Both panels ofFigure 1also demonstrate that financial institution and hedge fund blocks have become more common, whereas individual and other private entity blocks have become less common. Figure 2(a)demonstrates the overall trend in block diversity over time. This figure demonstrates that the prevalence of block diversity is quite common throughout our sample and that the median number of block types is close to two.Figure 2(b)shows that the average number of block types tends to be quite stable over the firm’s life cycle. Figure 3portrays a richer story. Specifically, this figure reports for each sample year the number of firms that have exactly one block versus those that have at least two blocks;Figure 3, (a)–(c), restricts the comparison with firms that have at least two, three, and four blocks, respectively. All three panels demonstrate that block diversity has been decreasing over time, and this is especially pronounced in firms with a large number of blocks ((b) and (c)). For example,Figure 3(b), which is restricted to firms with at least three blocks (52% of the sample according to Panel B ofTable 1), clearly shows that firms with a heterogeneous blockholder base (i.e., firms held by at least two block types) are becoming less common, whereas firms with a homogeneous blockholder base (i.e., firms held by exactly one block type) are becoming more common. The panels ofFigure 3suggest that blockholders avoid coexisting with blockholders of a different type, a conclusion supported by the findings ofHadlock and Schwartz-Ziv (2019), who show that financial and nonfinancial blocks repel each other. In sum, although the number of blocks in firms has been increasing over time, block diversity has been decreasing. These seemingly contradictory trends motivate us to investigate whether a more homogeneous blockholder base enhances or is detrimental to firm performance. We address this question in the next section.
article.3. Block Diversity and Firm Performance: In this section, we address the question of whether a diverse blockholder base is detrimental to firm performance. To investigate this question, we examine three distinct settings in which an exogenous variation due to (the revelation of) changes in block diversity potentially affects firm performance. 3.1. Abnormal Returns Around the Revelation of Block Diversity The first setting we examine to understand whether block diversity boosts or hurts firm performance focuses on the abnormal returns around the filing dates of 13G filings. Each shareholder who becomes a blockholder by acquiring at least 5% of the firm’s outstanding shares is required to disclose such an acquisition by filing either a 13G filing or a 13G filing, as specified inSection 2.2. Our data set includes 200,741 blocks disclosed in 13G filings, 31,567 blocks disclosed in 13D filings, and 8,250 blocks disclosed in form 3, 4, or 5 but not disclosed in a 13D filing or a 13G filing. Thus, the majority of the blockholders self-identify as 13G shareholders. Blockholders who hold at most 10% of the firm’s outstanding shares are permitted to file a 13G filing up to 45 days after the end of the calendar year in which they bought the stock, that is, up to February 14 of the following year, which may create a reporting lag of up to 13.5 months.Figure 4(a)reports the number of 13G filings filed during the 1998–2016 period, broken down by week. As this figure demonstrates, around weeks 5–7, we observe a peak in the number of 13G schedules filed. Because these weeks are all in February, inFigure 4(b), we focus on the month of February and break down the number of 13G filings by the calendar day. Here we observe that the frequency of the 13G filings builds up toward February 14, at which point it peaks and drops shortly thereafter. In fact, asFigure 4, (a) and (b), demonstrates, approximately two-thirds (61.34%) of all the 13G filings are filed around February 14, that is, between February 1 and 17, hereafter termed the “February window.” We include February 15–17 in our window because we observe inFigure 4(b)that 13G filings are still filed relatively frequently during these dates; however, excluding these dates does not significantly alter our results. Thus, we indeed observe a clustering of the 13G filings on, or around, the February 14 deadline, which we consider to be an exogenously predetermined date. Moreover, 90.6% of the blockholders filealltheir filings either during the February window or alternatively immediately after the block purchase, demonstrating that blockholders typically do not strategically select when to file the 13G filing depending on the specific firm. Put differently, although blocks are purchased throughout the year, the 13G filings are frequently filed only months later during the February window, on a random predetermined date. Thus, blockholders are permitted to disclose their new position with a delay that ranges between 45 and 410 calendar days. Most companies (88%) do not disclose the exact date they actually purchased the block, but if block purchases are reasonably equally distributed throughout the year, the average reporting lag would equal 227.5 calendar days ((45 + 410)/2), which is a substantial lag. This delay is ideal for identification purposes because it allows disentangling factors that may have contributed to the blockholder’s decision to become a blockholder. This finding suggests that the market views an increase in block diversity as being detrimental to firm performance. Because of this lag, we can attribute the market’s response to the 13G filings filed in the February window specifically to the revelation of the new blockholder composition (if this information has not been disclosed in a prior filing). Thus, the unique setting analyzed in Panel A ofTable 2is ideal for identification purposes because it allows disentangling the market’s response to the discovery that a new blockholder composition exists due to other events (e.g., a significant regulatory change) that may have not only endogenously triggered the entry of the new block, but also affected firm performance. Our results show that an increase in block diversity (i.e., havingotherblock types in a company), as opposed to an increase in the number of blocks, is detrimental to firm performance. CARs capture the short-term abnormal return (as noted inBall and Brown (1968)andHaleblian et al. (2009)) upon revelation of a new blockholder base. Online Appendix B demonstrates that following the entry of a new block, it typically takes at least two years for different block types to leave the firm. During this period, the different block types may attempt to get along, but these attempts may ultimately not always be successful. Until block diversity dissolves, firms may incur costs that arise from having a diverse blockholder base. Expectations for these costs are captured in the CARs measured inTable 2. Thus, we conclude that our evidence indicates that when the market learns that a firm’s blockholder base has become more diverse, the market responds significantly more negatively compared with when it learns that a homogeneous block composition has been maintained. The idea behind this approach is that if the entering block has already been or is still a blockholder in a company of the same industry, the blockholder will have relevant knowledge/skills. Overall, the Panel D specifications show that the combination of having block diversity and a blockholder with prior industry experience compensates, to some extent, for the cost of having (only) block diversity.17Nevertheless, block diversity remains detrimental to firm performance. Finally, the analysis described in this section is confined to 13G blocks (as opposed to 13D blocks), which are the most common block filers: 86.5% of the blocks are disclosed in a 13G filing, whereas only 13.5% of the blocks are disclosed in a 13D filing. 13D blockholders state that they acquired the block “with the purpose or effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer,” whereas 13G filers do not commit to this. A blockholder can typically choose whether, from his perspective, he is required to file a 13D or a 13G.18Financial institutions, which typically file 13Gs (rather than 13Ds), repeatedly declare in the governance reports they publish that they actively govern the firms they hold.19Overall, 13G blocks are likely to be somewhat less active than 13D blocks, but they nevertheless tend to be active and involved. In sum, in this section, we find support forHypothesis 1a, indicating that block diversity is detrimental to firm performance. 3.2. Is Block Diversity Detrimental Across the Board? There is quite a substantial literature that documents that in corporations, one size does not fit all, meaning that a certain situation may be beneficial for one type of company but not for a different type of company. For example, with respect to boards, studies have documented that a certain board size or board composition may be ideal for one type of company but not for another (Boone et al. 2007,Coles et al. 2008,Linck et al. 2008,Adams and Ferreira 2009,Ferreira 2010,Bernile et al. 2018). These studies suggest that also in our setting one size may not fit all, meaning that block diversity may not be universally detrimental foralltypes of firms. We address this possibility in Panel A ofTable 4. We include in this analysis different interaction terms that each control for a certain firm characteristic. In Model 1, this interaction variable isFirm size above median. We focus on this variable because prior studies have shown that for simple (complicated) tasks, a homogeneous (heterogeneous) team will maximize team performance (Jehn et al. 1999,Bowers et al. 2000). To classify which companies are more likely to deal with complicated tasks, we follow prior studies (Linck et al. 2008) and assume that smaller (larger) firms operate in less (more) complex environments. We use the firm’s market capitalization to estimate the firm size. In sum, we find that block diversity is particularly detrimental to two types of firms: small and relatively simple firms (small firms and/or firms with short 10-K filings) and companies that face high uncertainty levels (i.e., high volatility). By contrast, in large and complex firms, and in firms that face low levels of uncertainly, increasing block diversity is neutral. Thus, overall block diversity is detrimental; we do not find any subsample for which block diversity is beneficial, but block diversity is neutral for a nonnegligible portion of the firms. Because block diversity is detrimental for a substantial portion of the firms, we may conclude that, on aggregate, block diversity is detrimental.

(2022) show that shareholders with different opinions repel each other, andHadlock and Schwartz-Ziv (2019)show that different types of blocks repel each other. These studies all suggest that block diversity can generate friction or conflict. We next investigate whether this is indeed the case. 4.1. Block Diversity and Disagreement at Shareholder Votes The first setting we investigate to address the question of whether block diversity increases conflict among shareholders is shareholder votes. Each year at least one shareholder meeting is held in which shareholders are requested to vote on various topics. We examine whether shareholders’ votes indicate that they are more likely to disagree with each other and with management, as the blockholder base becomes more diverse. As we demonstrate in this section, we find support for this possibility. We start by examining how consensus among shareholders with respect to votes cast at shareholder meetings varies depending on block diversity. For any given vote, the percentage of votes cast consistent with management recommendation can range between 0% and 100%. At these exact two extreme points, shareholders are in complete consensus because all shareholders cast an identical vote. As the votes shift away from these two extreme points, consensus decreases and reaches the lowest possible point when half of the voted shares conflict with the other half of the voted shares, that is, 50% of the votes cast are consistent with management recommendation, whereas 50% are not. Following this rationale, we create aConsensus among shareholdersmeasure. AsFigure 6(a)demonstrates, this measure is defined as the absolute value of the (fraction that voted with management, 0.5). As this figure demonstrates, the majority of the vote outcomes are highly consistent with management recommendation, but some variation does exist. Thus, most of the disagreement captured in theConsensus among shareholdersmeasure is driven by votes that are close to being completely supportive of management (mostly above 90% support rate) but are not completely supportive of management. The submission of a shareholder proposal can serve as an indicator for increased disagreement between shareholders and management because submitting a proposal indicates that shareholders were not able to resolve in private the issue pertaining to the proposal. This specification is at the firm-year level. Indeed, column (3) demonstrates that if theNumber of block typesincreases by one unit, shareholders are expected to submit an additional 0.0332 proposals, which relative to the mean annual number of shareholder proposals submitted (0.231 proposals per year) reflects a 14.37% increase. Hence, this result indicates that an increase in block diversity is expected to lead to shareholders attempting to publicly resolve governance-related issues. In column (4) ofTable 5, we zoom in our analysis to the shareholder level and analyze votes cast by mutual funds. We investigate whether funds are more likely to vote against management when a block that is of a different type, that is, a nonfinancial block, is present in the firm’s blockholder base. We conduct this analysis because if block diversity indeed increases disagreement, we expect to find that when financial institutions vote (even when they are not blockholders), more disagreement will prevail when a different type of blockholder is present, that is, a nonfinancial block. Thus, we examine how the presence of a nonfinancial block is related to a fund voting against management recommendation, which we have shown previously, is also a close proxy for shareholders disagreeing with each other. Accordingly, we define the dependent variableFund voted with managementas an indicator variable equal to one if the fund voted in line with the management recommendation. The primary independent variable,Nonfinancial block, is an indicator variable equal to one if the firm’s blockholder base includes a nonfinancial block. In addition to the previously noted controls, the specification includes fund fixed effects, and it is conducted at the fund-proposal level. As reported in column (4), we find a negative and significant coefficient forNonfinancial block,indicating that when a nonfinancial block is present, funds are 7.38% more likely to vote against management,24that is, at a meaningful rate. Put differently, this finding demonstrates that shareholders, in this case financial institutions, are significantly more likely to disagree with management and therefore also with each other when a different type of blockholder is present in the firm’s blockholder base. 4.2. Block Diversity and Lawsuits Filed by Shareholders The second setting we investigate to address the question of whether increased conflict arises as block diversity increases is the legal setting. A lawsuit can indicate that a conflict has arisen, and the parties involved are not able to resolve the conflict independently. The existence of a conflict among shareholders that leads to litigation introduces uncertainty, and such increased uncertainty is typically viewed as being costly in corporate finance (Lowry and Shu 2002,Cao and Narayanamoorthy 2011). We examine whether lawsuits filed by a shareholder involving blockholders are more common when the blockholder base is diverse. If block diversity induces disagreement, we should observe that blockholders are more likely to sue the firm when its blockholder base is diverse. Our reasoning is that if the firm sides with the views of a first type of blockholder, and a second type of blockholder disagrees with the views of the first type of blockholder, the blockholders are more likely to find themselves in conflict and, ultimately, one of the blocks, or shareholders affected by the conflict, will sue the firm. From our own analysis of the lawsuits included in the legal data set described later and from discussions with legal experts, we have observed and learned that when shareholders disagree with each other, they will sue the firm (which apparently sides with the views of one of the shareholders), but that shareholders are not likely to sue each other. For this reason, our analysis focuses on blockholders and shareholders suing the firm. To analyze whether blockholders and shareholders are more likely to sue the firm when the blockholder base is diverse and a blockholder is involved in the dispute, we manually assemble a unique data set documenting such lawsuits. We assemble this data set using the legal Westlaw database, which includes data on lawsuits filed, and is widely used by law academics. To identify potential lawsuits filed by a shareholder and/or a blockholder, we focus on the opinion documents. A judicial opinion is a legal document written by a judge or a judicial panel in the course of resolving a legal dispute, providing a description of the facts leading to the dispute, the decision reached to resolve the dispute, and an analysis of the law used to support the decision. To select lawsuits filed by shareholders, we follow two screening steps. In the first screening step we define an algorithm that identifies lawsuits that potentially involve shareholders and/or blockholders. The algorithm was defined after reading and analyzing several dozens of lawsuits and identifying words that frequently appear in lawsuits filed by shareholders that involve a blockholder. The algorithm requires that the judicial opinion mention the company name in the title of the lawsuit and the term “fiduciary duty” or “fiduciary duties” at least once in the judicial opinion itself.25This first screening step allows us to identify 2,574 judicial opinions. In the second screening step, we manually examine each of the lawsuits identified in the first step and verify that the lawsuit was indeed filed by a shareholder. We also require that at least one of the blockholder’s names be mentioned in the judicial opinion document because this indicates that the blockholder is involved in the lawsuit. We identify 368 lawsuits that fall under the algorithm’s second screening step. We acknowledge that our algorithm may be too stringent and that therefore we may not have identified all lawsuits filed by shareholders. However, this limitation should bias our results downward, as we are not identifying all shareholder-initiated lawsuits that involve a blockholder. The lawsuits filed by shareholders can revolve around different issues, but perhaps two themes are particularly prevalent in these lawsuits. The first and most common theme is shareholders alleging that violations of SEC regulations occurred. These violations are often labeled as fraud, breach of fiduciary duty, or negligent misrepresentation. An example of such a lawsuit is the lawsuit filed initially by Lester Goldstein (a shareholder) against Cendant Corporation. In this lawsuit, the shareholders allege that the corporation and its directors conducted fraud. The shareholders argue that this fraud ultimately led to the financial reports being restated and to a sharp decrease in the stock price. A second theme that appears in these lawsuits is that of minority shareholders suing majority shareholders for illegally using their collective power to cancel out the voice of minority shareholders. An example of such a lawsuit is the lawsuit filed by Rebecca Proctor (a shareholder) against Vishay Intertechnology, Inc., in which the minority shareholder claim that the majority shareholders breached their fiduciary duty, leading to a “waste [of] corporate assets,” and that minority shareholders did not receive the fair value of the stocks they held when the company was acquired. InTable 6, we report our analysis of whether firms with a more diverse blockholder base are more likely to have a shareholder filing a lawsuit against the firm, as is revealed in the judicial opinion written by the judge. The analysis is conducted at the firm-year level. The dependent variable in this table isLawsuit filed, which is an indicator variable that is equal to one if an opinion (i.e., a verdict) was written by the judge on a lawsuit filed by a shareholder and zero otherwise. The following vector of controls is included: number of blocks, past year abnormal return, market capitalization, firm age, idiosyncratic volatility, total percentage of block ownership, total percentage of institutional ownership, fixed assets, capital expenditures, leverage, and the Amihud illiquidity measure. Two potential concerns arise: (a) block diversity can exist only if firms have at least two blocks, and (b) it is not clear whether firms with no blocks should be defined as firms with a diverse or nondiverse blockholder base. We obtain almost identical results. Thus, the results demonstrate that, in firms with a diverse blockholder base, disagreements are more likely to arise, as reflected in the elevated frequency of lawsuits filed by shareholders.
article.5. Block Diversity and Firm Performance: Thus far, we focused on various settings in which exogenous shocks to firms alter or reveal block diversity to isolate factors that affect firm performance from factors that affect blockholder composition. In the present section, we investigate the relation between block diversity and financial performance in the complete data set rather than focusing our attention on a subset of observations of firms experiencing an exogenous shock from (the revelation of) block ownership. Although such an analysis does not point to a causal relation, it does demonstrate the relation between block diversity and financial performance for the full data set, and thus, at minimum, it allows us to conclude whether block diversity is associated with stronger or weaker financial performance. 5.1. Portfolio Returns In this section, we examine the possibility that firms with a diverse blockholder base underperform relative to firms with a homogeneous blockholder base by constructing such corresponding portfolios. Thus, we construct portfolios that take a long position on firms with a homogeneous blockholder base (i.e., firms held by exactly one type of block) and a short position on firms with a heterogeneous blockholder base (i.e., firms held by at least two types of blocks). We exclude from this analysis firms not held by any type of block at all, even though including such firms by defining them as firms with a homogeneous blockholder base would not alter the results substantially. We construct the portfolios based on the block composition at the beginning of each calendar year and determine the weights in the portfolios based on the market capitalization at the beginning of each calendar year. Each specification is estimated using monthly returns. The average abnormal returns are constructed as the intercept (alphas) of a time series regression of portfolio returns based on the Fama–French four-factor model. In the reported results,Market premiumis the market premium,SMBis small market capitalization minus big,HMLis high book-to-market ratio minus low, andUMDis the monthly momentum. Robust standard errors are reported. The results are reported inTable 7. Columns (1)–(3) report results for value-weighted portfolios, whereas columns (4)–(6) report results for equal-weighted portfolios. As the results in Model 1 (4) indicate, a value-weighted (equal-weighted) portfolio that takes a long position in firms with a homogeneous blockholder base (as described previously) yields a monthly alpha equal to an insignificant 0.06% (significant 0.38%) abnormal return. Column (2) ((5)) reports results for a portfolio that takes a short position in firms with a heterogeneous blockholder base and reports a significant −0.25% (−0.64%) abnormal monthly return indicating that firms with a heterogeneous blockholder base underperform in a four-factor model. Column (3) ((6)) reports estimates for a portfolio that combines the corresponding long/short portfolios reported in columns (1) and (2) ((4) and (5)). Such a portfolio is expected to yield a significant monthly abnormal return equal to 0.31% (0.88%), equivalent to a 3.78% (11.08%) annual abnormal return. Once again, in unreported specifications we repeat the analysis for firms that have exactly one block and at least two blocks and obtain economically very similar results. Although these results do not necessarily point to a causal relation between block diversity and firm performance, they do clearly demonstrate that an investment strategy that takes a long position in firms with a homogeneous blockholder base, and a short position in firms with a heterogeneous blockholder base, is expected to yield substantial returns. This finding suggests that, at minimum, the former firms are more profitable than the latter. The negative direction of the market reaction we observe when an increase in block diversity is revealed (as analyzed inSection 3.1) is consistent with the negative direction of the market reaction we subsequently observe in this section in the long-term return portfolio analysis. This finding is consistent with a large literature that examines the price drift following a certain event. This literature has frequently documented that after an event occurs the stock price continues to drift in the same direction as the CAR observed on the event date. This phenomenon is described inBall and Brown (1968)with respect to earnings announcements, as cited in byBernard and Thomas (1989, p. 1): “Even after earnings are announced, estimated cumulative ‘abnormal’ returns continue to drift up for ‘good news’ firms and down for ‘bad news’ firms.”Bernard and Thomas (1989)andDesai and Jain (1997)further demonstrate this phenomenon.26 Thus, our findings are consistent with prior studies that have documented the phenomenon of the market response on the event date being in the same direction as the subsequent price drift/long-term performance, implying that the market’s initial response does not fully capture the magnitude of the impact of the event. 5.2. article.6. Conclusions: We document that firms frequently have diverse types of blockholders in the firm’s blockholder base. Our analysis consistently demonstrates that block diversity is followed by, and is associated with, weaker financial performance. In addition, we document increased disagreement among shareholders when different types of blockholders are present in the firm’s blockholder base. Taken together, our results suggest that maintaining a heterogeneous blockholder base entails certain costs and that having multiple block types that each use different, and potentially conflicting, governance patterns may not be optimal for companies.
